channel.title: Class
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian

item.title: Complexity Index
item.description:
There are many types of complexity that show up, however they can be grouped into a few distinct categories.

Polynomial
Logarithmic
Linearithmic
Exponential

item.title: Constant Complexity
item.description: These operations allways take the same amount of time to compute. Regardless of the amount of inputs.

item.title: Exponential Complexity
item.description: Algorithms with with exponential complexity have a Big O of O(). This results in horrible scaling. Most algorithm with this complexity will become unusable before reaching the double digits.

item.title: Linear Complexity
item.description: These algorithms have a directly proportional relationship to amount of inputs given.

item.title: Logarithmic Complexity
item.description: For a logarithmic algorithm to increase the time taken by 1 unit you need to double the input data!

item.title: Polynomial Complexity
item.description:
Constant, linear, & quadratic algorithms are examples of polynomial algorithms.
The one uniting factor between these algorithms is that they all have a Big O of O() where is a constant value. It can be any value, even 0, as show by constant complexities.
In general these algorithms scale terribly, with O() (constant complexities) being the one exception.

item.title: Quadratic Complexity
item.description: If an input doubles, the time taken to solve it increases by 4 times!

item.title: Space Complexity
item.description:
Unlike other types of complexity space complexity doesn't concern the time an algorithm takes to compute. It represents the amount of memory an algorithm uses while running.
Most algorithms have a space complexity of O(1)|. For example linear search uses the same amount of memory, regardless of the amount of inputs given. It will always only store the current index in the array that is being checked & the value to search for.
However, algorithms that "divide & conquer" tent to high a higher space complexity

item.title: Binary search
item.description:
Binary search is a sorting algorithm that worst by checking the middle element of an array. If the middle element is higher, the top half of the array is "disguarded". If the middle element is lower, the bottom half of the array is "disguarded". This process repeates until the desired value is found.
This is very efficent for a sorted list, with a Big O of O(), since it has a logarithmic complexity.

item.title: Linear Search
item.description:
A linear search is an algorithm that iterates over every element in an array. Once it has iterated over every element it can return true if the array contains the desired element, otherwise false.
Effects of checking every element once:

Results in a Big O of O().
Enables linear search to work on unsorted arrays.

item.title: Searching Algorithms
item.description:
Searching algorithms are a set of algorithms that are used to find an element within an array.
Two examples of searching algorithms are linear search & binary search.
See [here](Binary and Linear Search Visualization (usfca.edu)) for a demenstartion of them at work. Make sure input a number in the top left & select linear or binary search.

item.title: Bubble Sort
item.description:
This algorithm iterates over an array, comparing adjacent elements. If the elements are out of order they are swapped. This produces an effect where the highest element is "bubbled" to the end of the array on each iteration.
The algorithm performs two comparisons per switch so therefore it has a Big O of O(); this makes it a quadratic algorithm.

Fortunately, there are two optimizations that can be made to bubble sort.

If no swaps were performed on the last iteration then the list is sorted, so exit early.
For each iteration of the loop, you don't need to check any elements after the size minus the iteration index. As those elements have already been sorted by previous iterations.
This changes the bubble sort best vs worst complexity to:

Best case: O()
Worst case: O()
Click here for a demonstration of the algorithm at work. (The link only shows an optimised version of bubble sort).

item.title: Merge Sort
item.description:
Merge sort works by recursively splitting the array into smaller sub-arrays until each array contains only one element. It then builds back up the initial array from the sub-arrays, sorting each time a sub-array is combined.
Big O of O(), this is a linearithmic algorithm.

item.title: Sorting-Algorithms
item.description:
Sorting algorithms are algorithms that take an unsorted array & convert it to a "sorted" array.
There is no hard defention for what constitutes an array as sorted. It is entirely context dependent. However, a sorted array is usually regarded as a set of numbers in assending order or a collection of words in alphabetical order.
Examples of sorting algorithms:

Bubble sort
Merge sort

item.title: Big O Notation
item.description:
Big O notation is a representation of how well an algorithm scales on more elements are given to the algorithm.
This notation is written in the form O() where symbolises the amount of elements given to an algorithm.

Take for example a Linear algorithm. This type of algorithm has a Big O of O(). The execution time increases directly proportionally with the amount of input.
On my computer a linear algorithm with 10 inputs may take 3 seconds to compute. Yet, the same algorithm with the same input may take 1 second to compute on your computer. If the input is doubled to 20 then the algorithm will take 6 seconds on my computer & 2 seconds.
Even though my computer is slower than your computer the time taken doubles with double the inputs!
Therefore, using Big O provides an easy way to compare algorithms across machines of different performance.

When stating the Big O of an algorithm only the dominant term is included, this is because the terms that aren't dominant quickly become inconsequential as the number of inputs scales higher & higher.
To get the dominant term of an expression you just get the term that grows the fastest. For example, in the equation the dominant term would be as & don't grow as fast. The constant of can be ignored as it also doesn't grow as fast. Then what's left is just . This is the complexity of this algorithm.

When running an algorithm sometimes it's Big O notiation can vary based on the starting conditions. The starting conditions are usually split into best & worst case.
For example in bubble sort.

The best case scenario is that everything is already sorted. This would have a Big O of O().
The worse case scenario is that everything is in a reversed order. This would have a Big O of O().
When comparing algorithms the worst case scenario is always used.

item.title: Index:
item.description:
Big O Notation
Searching algorithms
Sorting algorithms
Space complexity
